defense.layerIntegrityDetails: HMAC-SHA256 verification on all vault files

defense.layerAuditDetails: Append-only signed log with SHA-256 chain
defense.cryptoCiDesc: 256-bit AEAD cipher with nonce-misuse resistance ensures data stays private and unmodified
defense.cryptoAvailDesc: Offline-first architecture with redundant local storage ensures access even without network
defense.cryptoFsDesc: Ephemeral key exchange ensures past sessions cannot be decrypted if long-term keys are compromised
defense.cryptoNrDesc: Cryptographic signatures prove authenticity and prevent denial of vault operations

srp.name: SRP-6a Zero-Knowledge Auth
srp.desc: Password never leaves device. Server verifies without knowing password.

fido2.name: FIDO2 / Biometric MFA

aead.name: AEAD File Encryption
aead.desc: Authenticated encryption with associated data for all vault contents.

pinning.name: Certificate Pinning
pinning.desc: HTTPS connections validated against known server certificate pins.

integrity.name: Device Integrity
integrity.desc: Runtime detection of jailbreak, root, debugger, and instrumentation.
integrity.details: Jailbreak, root, debugger, emulator, Frida, Xposed detection

runtime.name: Runtime Protection
runtime.desc: Screenshot blocking, secure pasteboard, memory cleanup.

selfdestruct.name: Self-Destruct / Brute-Force
selfdestruct.desc: Vault wipes after configurable failed login attempts.

audit.name: Audit Trail
audit.desc: Cryptographically signed log of all security-relevant actions.
audit.details: Append-only log, HMAC-SHA256 integrity, tamper detection

ghost.name: Ghost Mode / Anti-Forensics
ghost.desc: RAM scrubbing, clipboard sanitization, trace elimination.

pqc.name: Post-Quantum Cryptography
pqc.desc: ML-KEM-1024 hybrid key encapsulation for quantum resistance.

ci.name: Confidentiality & Integrity
ci.desc: Every file in your vault is encrypted with AEAD ciphers that bind ciphertext to an authentication tag — if even one bit is altered, decryption fails. This guarantees that only you (with the correct key) can read your data, and any tampering is immediately detected.

availability.name: Availability
availability.desc: Your vault stays accessible even under adverse conditions. Encrypted backups, BIP39 recovery phrases, and multi-vault architecture ensure you can always recover your data — even if a device is lost, stolen, or wiped by the self-destruct policy.
availability.algo: AES-256-GCM encrypted backups · BIP39 24-word recovery · Multi-vault redundancy

fs.name: Forward Secrecy
fs.desc: Each secure share and message session derives a unique ephemeral key pair. Even if your long-term identity key is compromised in the future, all past conversations and file transfers remain encrypted and unreadable.
fs.algo: X25519 ephemeral key exchange · Per-session ECDH · HKDF-SHA256 key derivation

nr.name: Non-Repudiation
nr.desc: Every vault operation is digitally signed with your Ed25519 identity key, creating a cryptographic proof of authorship. The post-quantum ML-DSA-87 co-signature future-proofs these proofs against quantum attacks on the audit trail.
nr.algo: Ed25519 identity signatures · ML-DSA-87 (FIPS 204) co-signatures · HMAC-SHA256 audit chain

classroom.pageSubtitle: Learn about post-quantum cryptography and security
classroom.keyManagementDesc: Best practices for encryption key lifecycle
classroom.lesson1Desc: Learn the basics of symmetric and asymmetric encryption
classroom.lesson2Desc: Understanding lattice-based cryptography and NIST standards
classroom.lesson3Desc: Creating strong passwords and protecting your vault access
classroom.lesson4Desc: Securing your USB drive and encrypted vault
classroom.lesson6Desc: Backup strategies and data recovery best practices
classroom.lesson7Desc: Detecting and responding to security threats
classroom.cryptoLabSubtitle: Try encrypting text with different ciphers, then try to break them.
classroom.bruteCaesarTitle: All 26 Caesar shifts (one is your plaintext):

section1.title: What Is Encryption?
section1.body: Encryption transforms readable data (plaintext) into an unreadable format (ciphertext) using a mathematical algorithm and a secret key. Only someone with the correct key can reverse the process (decryption) and recover the original data.

section2.title: Symmetric vs Asymmetric
section2.body: Symmetric encryption uses the same key for both encryption and decryption (e.g., AES-256). Asymmetric encryption uses a key pair — a public key to encrypt and a private key to decrypt (e.g., RSA, X25519). USBVault uses symmetric AEAD ciphers for vault data and asymmetric key exchange for secure sharing.

section3.title: AEAD: Authenticated Encryption
section3.body: AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data) provides both confidentiality and integrity in a single operation. AES-256-GCM-SIV and XChaCha20-Poly1305 are AEAD ciphers — they encrypt your data AND produce an authentication tag that detects any tampering. If even one bit is modified, decryption fails entirely.

section4.title: Key Derivation
section4.body: Your password is never used directly as an encryption key. Instead, a Key Derivation Function (KDF) like Argon2id stretches your password into a cryptographically strong key. Argon2id is memory-hard, meaning it requires significant RAM to compute — this makes brute-force attacks with GPUs or ASICs economically impractical.

lesson1.takeaway: Modern encryption with AEAD ciphers provides both secrecy and tamper detection. Your password is strengthened through Argon2id before it ever touches your data.

section1.title: The Quantum Threat
section1.body: Quantum computers use qubits that can exist in superposition, enabling them to solve certain problems exponentially faster than classical computers. Shor's algorithm can break RSA and elliptic curve cryptography in polynomial time, threatening all public-key systems currently in use.

section2.title: What PQC Protects Against
section2.body: Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) uses mathematical problems believed to be hard for both classical AND quantum computers — primarily lattice-based problems. Even a future quantum computer with millions of stable qubits cannot efficiently solve these.

section3.title: ML-KEM and ML-DSA
section3.body: USBVault implements ML-KEM-1024 (Module-Lattice Key Encapsulation) for key exchange and ML-DSA-87 (Module-Lattice Digital Signatures) for authentication. These are NIST-standardized (FIPS 203/204) and represent the strongest post-quantum security levels available.

section4.title: Harvest Now, Decrypt Later
section4.body: Adversaries may be recording encrypted traffic today, planning to decrypt it once quantum computers mature. This "harvest now, decrypt later" strategy is why migrating to PQC is urgent even though large-scale quantum computers don't exist yet. USBVault's PQC layer protects your data against this future threat.

lesson2.takeaway: Quantum computers will break today's public-key crypto. USBVault uses NIST-standardized lattice-based algorithms (ML-KEM, ML-DSA) to protect your data against both current and future threats.

section1.title: Why Passwords Still Matter
section1.body: Even with the strongest encryption, your vault is only as secure as the password protecting it. A weak password can be guessed through dictionary attacks, credential stuffing, or brute-force in minutes. Your password is the front door — everything else is walls.

section2.title: Zero-Knowledge Authentication (SRP-6a)
section2.body: USBVault uses the Secure Remote Password protocol (SRP-6a) so your password NEVER leaves your device — not even as a hash. The server verifies you know the password through a mathematical proof without ever seeing it. If the server is compromised, attackers gain nothing useful.

section3.title: What Makes a Strong Password
section3.body: Entropy is what matters — the number of possible combinations an attacker must try. A 4-word passphrase from a 7,776-word list (Diceware) has ~51 bits of entropy. A 6-word passphrase has ~77 bits — enough to resist offline brute-force attacks for centuries. Length beats complexity: "correct-horse-battery-staple" is stronger than "P@$$w0rd!".

section4.title: Multi-Factor Authentication
section4.body: Passwords alone aren't enough. USBVault supports FIDO2 hardware keys (YubiKey, Titan) and biometric locks as second factors. Hardware keys are phishing-resistant — they cryptographically verify the website's identity, so they cannot be tricked by lookalike domains.

lesson3.takeaway: Use a long passphrase (4+ random words), enable FIDO2/biometric MFA, and trust that SRP-6a means your password never leaves your device.

section1.title: USB Threat Landscape
section1.body: USB devices are one of the most common attack vectors. BadUSB attacks reprogram a drive's firmware to impersonate a keyboard and inject malicious commands. Rubber Ducky attacks execute payloads in seconds. Even trusted drives can be compromised through supply-chain attacks.

section2.title: How USBVault Protects Your Drive
section2.body: USBVault creates an encrypted vault partition on your USB drive. All data is encrypted with AES-256-GCM-SIV before being written to disk. The vault is locked by default — without your password + optional hardware key, the data is indistinguishable from random noise.

section3.title: Secure Initialization
section3.body: When you set up a USB vault, USBVault generates fresh cryptographic keys, writes encrypted metadata headers, and optionally performs a secure wipe of existing data. The setup wizard guides you through algorithm selection (AES-GCM-SIV vs XChaCha20), key size, and backup configuration.

section4.title: Safe Removal
section4.body: Always use USBVault's "Lock Vault" before physically removing your drive. This ensures all pending writes are flushed, encryption buffers are zeroed from memory, and the vault lock state is persisted. Removing a drive while unlocked could leave decrypted fragments in the OS file cache.

lesson4.takeaway: USB drives are high-risk attack surfaces. USBVault encrypts everything at rest, and you should always lock your vault before removing the drive.

section1.title: Never Trust, Always Verify
section1.body: Zero Trust is a security model that assumes no user, device, or network is inherently trustworthy — even inside a corporate perimeter. Every access request must be authenticated, authorized, and encrypted. USBVault applies this principle: every file operation requires proof of identity.

section2.title: Certificate Pinning
section2.body: USBVault pins the exact TLS certificates it expects from its servers. Even if an attacker compromises a Certificate Authority and issues a fraudulent certificate, the app will reject the connection. This defeats man-in-the-middle attacks from compromised networks, corporate proxies, and nation-state adversaries.

section3.title: Runtime Integrity Checks
section3.body: On every launch, USBVault verifies its own integrity — checking for debugger attachment, jailbreak/root detection, code signature verification, and memory guard validation. If any check fails, the app enters a degraded mode that protects your data from potentially compromised environments.

section4.title: Defense-in-Depth
section4.body: No single security layer is perfect. USBVault implements 10 overlapping layers — from SRP-6a authentication through PQC encryption to tamper-evident audit logs. If one layer is bypassed, the remaining layers continue to protect your data. Visit the Defense-in-Depth screen to see all 10 layers and their status.

lesson5.takeaway: Zero Trust means verifying everything, trusting nothing. USBVault applies this through certificate pinning, runtime integrity checks, and 10 overlapping defense layers.

section1.title: Why Backups Are Critical
section1.body: Encrypted data is especially vulnerable to loss — if you lose your encryption key or your storage medium fails, the data is gone forever. Unlike unencrypted data, there's no "recovery service" that can help. Backups are your only insurance policy.

section2.title: USBVault Backup System
section2.body: USBVault's backup system (FEAT-07) creates complete snapshots of your vault data, file metadata, and settings. Backups can be exported as encrypted files (using the same AES-256-GCM-SIV as your vault) or stored unencrypted for maximum recoverability. Encrypted backups use a separate passphrase via Argon2id key derivation.

section3.title: Auto-Backup Scheduling
section3.body: Configure automatic backups on daily, weekly, or monthly schedules. USBVault retains the last 3, 5, or 10 backups (configurable) and automatically prunes older ones. The backup history screen shows every backup with its timestamp, size, and integrity status.

section4.title: Restore Process
section4.body: To restore, import a backup file and provide the decryption passphrase if encrypted. USBVault validates the backup's integrity before restoring, checking metadata, vault counts, and structural validity. You can choose to merge with existing data or perform a clean restore.

lesson6.takeaway: With encryption, data loss is permanent. Configure auto-backups, store backup files in a separate location, and test your restore process periodically.

section1.title: Tamper-Evident Audit Trail
section1.body: Every vault operation — file additions, deletions, access events, setting changes, login attempts — is logged to an append-only audit trail. Entries are chained using SHA-256 hashes, creating a tamper-evident log. If any entry is modified or deleted, the hash chain breaks and the tampering is detected.

section2.title: Brute-Force Protection
section2.body: USBVault implements exponential backoff on failed login attempts. After configurable threshold failures, the account enters a progressive lockout with increasing delays. The Self-Destruct layer (optional) can trigger a complete vault wipe after a set number of failed attempts — the ultimate protection against physical theft.

section3.title: Ghost Mode
section3.body: When Ghost Mode is active, USBVault performs a zero-trace cleanup on lock or logout — clearing browser caches, session tokens, temporary files, clipboard data, and any forensic residue. This is designed for high-risk environments where leaving no trace of vault activity is critical.

section4.title: File Integrity Verification
section4.body: Every encrypted file includes an HMAC-SHA256 authentication tag computed over the ciphertext and associated metadata. On decryption, the tag is verified first — if it doesn't match, the file is flagged as corrupted or tampered and decryption is refused. This prevents silent data corruption from going undetected.

lesson7.takeaway: USBVault detects threats through hash-chained audit logs, brute-force lockouts, HMAC integrity verification, and optional zero-trace cleanup via Ghost Mode.

section1.title: What Digital Forensics Can Recover
section1.body: Forensic tools can recover deleted files from disk sectors, extract encryption keys from memory dumps, reconstruct browsing history from OS caches, and correlate timestamps across log files. Even "deleted" data often persists until the physical sectors are overwritten.

section2.title: How USBVault Defends Against Forensics
section2.body: USBVault's File Shredder performs multi-pass overwrites (DoD 5220.22-M standard) to prevent sector-level recovery. Memory buffers containing decrypted data are zeroed immediately after use. The Zero-Trace Cleanup scans for and eliminates forensic artifacts across 8 categories of system traces.

section3.title: Secure Deletion Limitations
section3.body: On modern SSDs, secure deletion is complicated by wear-leveling algorithms that distribute writes across flash cells. The SSD controller may keep old copies of data in cells marked for garbage collection. For maximum security on SSDs, full-disk encryption (where USBVault operates) is more effective than per-file secure deletion because the encryption key can be destroyed.

section4.title: Operational Security (OpSec)
section4.body: Technology alone isn't enough. Practice good OpSec: don't access your vault on untrusted computers, use a VPN on public networks, keep your OS and USBVault updated, and be aware of shoulder-surfing. The strongest encryption is worthless if someone watches you type your password.

lesson8.takeaway: Forensic recovery is powerful, but USBVault counters it with multi-pass shredding, memory zeroing, and zero-trace cleanup. Combine technical protections with good operational security habits.

caesar.name: Caesar Cipher
caesar.desc: Shift each letter by a fixed amount. The simplest cipher — breakable by trying all 26 keys.

xor.name: XOR Cipher
xor.desc: XOR each byte with a key. Fast but vulnerable to frequency analysis if key is short.

aesGcm.name: AES-256-GCM
aesGcm.desc: The gold standard. 256-bit key, authenticated encryption. Used by governments worldwide.

xchacha.name: XChaCha20-Poly1305
xchacha.desc: USBVault's default cipher. 24-byte nonce makes random generation safe. Used by Signal, WireGuard.

aesCbc.name: AES-CBC
aesCbc.desc: AES in Cipher Block Chaining mode with PKCS7 padding. Encrypts data in 16-byte blocks, each XORed with the previous ciphertext block.
aesCbc.takeaway: CBC doesn't authenticate — an attacker can flip bits undetected. Always prefer AEAD modes like GCM or Poly1305.

rsa.name: RSA-OAEP
rsa.desc: Public-key encryption using RSA with OAEP padding. Generate a key pair: encrypt with the public key, decrypt with the private key.
rsa.takeaway: Public key encrypts, private key decrypts — you can share your public key safely. RSA is slow, so it's used for key exchange, not bulk encryption.

chachaCompare.name: ChaCha20 vs XChaCha
chachaCompare.desc: Visual comparison of nonce sizes between ChaCha20-Poly1305 (12-byte nonce) and XChaCha20-Poly1305 (24-byte nonce).
chachaCompare.takeaway: XChaCha20's 24-byte nonce makes random nonce collisions virtually impossible — that's why USBVault uses it.

xor.result1: XOR with short key: vulnerable to frequency analysis
xor.result2: Key length detection via Hamming distance...
xor.result3: Once key length known: break each position independently
xor.result4: Lesson: XOR is NOT encryption without a random key as long as the message

aead.result3: That's longer than the age of the universe (13.8 × 10^9 years)
aead.result4: Brute-force is economically and physically impossible.
aead.result5: This is why USBVault uses these ciphers.

aesCbc.result1: AES-256 key space: 2^256 — brute-force is infeasible
aesCbc.result2: But CBC lacks authentication: a bit-flip attack can alter plaintext
aesCbc.result3: The padding oracle attack (Vaudenay, 2002) broke real CBC systems
aesCbc.result4: Lesson: Encryption without authentication is dangerous — use AEAD

rsa.result1: RSA-2048: factoring a 617-digit number — infeasible for classical computers
rsa.result2: Shor's algorithm could break RSA on a future quantum computer
rsa.result3: This is why USBVault uses post-quantum ML-KEM for key exchange

chachaCompare.result1: ChaCha20 (96-bit nonce): collision likely after ~2^48 messages with random nonces
chachaCompare.result2: XChaCha20 (192-bit nonce): collision unlikely until ~2^96 messages
chachaCompare.result3: For random nonces, XChaCha20 is always the safer choice

pbkdf2.name: PBKDF2
pbkdf2.desc: Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2. Repeatedly applies HMAC-SHA256 to stretch a password into a strong key. More iterations = more time for attackers to brute-force.

bcrypt.name: bcrypt
bcrypt.desc: Adaptive cost-factor hashing based on the Blowfish cipher. The cost factor doubles the work with each increment, making it future-proof as hardware gets faster.
bcrypt.explanation: bcrypt's cost factor is exponential: each +1 doubles computation time. Cost 12 is common today; increase as hardware improves.

scrypt.name: scrypt
scrypt.desc: Memory-hard key derivation that requires both CPU time AND large amounts of RAM, making GPU and ASIC attacks prohibitively expensive.
scrypt.explanation: scrypt's memory-hardness is the key advantage: GPUs have fast cores but limited memory per core, making parallel attacks expensive.

argon2i.name: Argon2i
argon2i.desc: Data-independent memory access pattern — resists timing side-channel attacks. Designed for environments where side-channel resistance is critical.
argon2i.explanation: Argon2i accesses memory in a pattern independent of the password, preventing cache-timing attacks that could leak information about the password.

argon2id.name: Argon2id
argon2id.desc: USBVault's choice. Hybrid: Argon2i for the first pass (side-channel resistant), then Argon2d (faster, data-dependent) for subsequent passes.
argon2id.explanation: Argon2id combines the best of both worlds: side-channel resistance from Argon2i's first pass and maximum GPU resistance from Argon2d's subsequent passes. USBVault uses: 64 MiB memory, 3 iterations, 4 parallel lanes.

hkdf.name: HKDF
hkdf.desc: HMAC-based Key Derivation Function. Extracts and expands a master key into multiple subkeys. Uses 'info' strings for domain separation — different purposes get different keys.
hkdf.subkeyDemo: Subkey derivation with domain separation — same master key, different purposes:

caesar.howItWorks: Each letter is shifted by a fixed number of positions in the alphabet. With a shift of 3, A becomes D, B becomes E, and so on. Decryption reverses the shift.
caesar.strengths: Simple to understand and implement. Great for teaching the concept of symmetric encryption where both parties share a secret (the shift number).
caesar.weaknesses: Only 25 possible keys — trivially broken by trying all shifts (brute force). Letter frequency patterns survive encryption, enabling frequency analysis attacks.
caesar.realWorld: Used by Julius Caesar for military messages (~58 BC). Today used only for educational purposes and simple puzzles (ROT13).

xor.howItWorks: Each byte of plaintext is combined with a corresponding byte of the key using the XOR (exclusive-or) operation. XOR is reversible: applying XOR twice with the same key restores the original.
xor.strengths: Extremely fast (single CPU instruction per byte). When key is as long as the message and truly random, it becomes a one-time pad — theoretically unbreakable.
xor.weaknesses: With short repeating keys, vulnerable to frequency analysis and known-plaintext attacks. Key reuse completely breaks security — XORing two ciphertexts cancels the key.
xor.realWorld: Foundation of modern stream ciphers (ChaCha20, RC4). Used in XOR-based obfuscation in malware and simple DRM schemes.

aesGcm.howItWorks: AES encrypts 128-bit blocks using a 256-bit key through 14 rounds of substitution and permutation. GCM (Galois/Counter Mode) adds authentication — it produces both ciphertext and a 128-bit authentication tag that detects any tampering.
aesGcm.strengths: NIST standardized and FIPS approved. Hardware-accelerated on most modern CPUs (AES-NI). Authenticated encryption (AEAD) prevents both eavesdropping and tampering in a single operation.
aesGcm.weaknesses: 96-bit nonce means collision risk after ~2^32 messages with the same key. Nonce reuse is catastrophic — reveals the authentication key. Not nonce-misuse resistant (unlike GCM-SIV).
aesGcm.realWorld: TLS 1.3 (HTTPS), Wi-Fi WPA3, government classified communications (Suite B), disk encryption (BitLocker, LUKS).

xchacha.howItWorks: ChaCha20 is a stream cipher that generates a keystream from a 256-bit key and nonce using 20 rounds of quarter-round operations. The 'X' variant extends the nonce from 12 to 24 bytes using HChaCha20, making random nonce generation safe. Poly1305 provides authentication.
xchacha.strengths: 192-bit nonce eliminates nonce collision concerns even with random generation. No timing side-channels (constant-time by design). Fast in software without hardware acceleration. Used by USBVault as the default cipher.
xchacha.weaknesses: Not FIPS approved (AES-256-GCM-SIV is USBVault's FIPS option). Less hardware acceleration support compared to AES on Intel/AMD CPUs.
xchacha.realWorld: WireGuard VPN, Signal Protocol, Google's QUIC (HTTP/3), Cloudflare's TLS stack, SSH (openssh), USBVault default encryption.

aesCbc.howItWorks: Each plaintext block is XORed with the previous ciphertext block before encryption (chaining). The first block uses a random Initialization Vector (IV). Plaintext must be padded to a multiple of 16 bytes (PKCS7).
aesCbc.strengths: Well-understood and widely deployed. Simpler to implement than GCM mode. Provides semantic security with a random IV.
aesCbc.weaknesses: No authentication — an attacker can flip ciphertext bits and cause predictable plaintext changes (bit-flipping attack). Padding oracle attacks (like POODLE) can decrypt data. Requires separate MAC for integrity.
aesCbc.realWorld: Legacy TLS (before 1.3), older disk encryption, AES-CBC + HMAC-SHA256 (encrypt-then-MAC) is still used in some systems. Being replaced by AEAD ciphers.

rsa.howItWorks: Based on the mathematical difficulty of factoring large numbers. A public key (n, e) encrypts; the private key (n, d) decrypts. Security relies on the fact that given n = p × q (two large primes), finding p and q is computationally infeasible.
rsa.strengths: Enables public-key cryptography — anyone can encrypt with your public key, only you can decrypt. Key exchange without a pre-shared secret. Digital signatures for authentication and non-repudiation.
rsa.weaknesses: Slow (1000x slower than AES for bulk data). Key sizes must be large (2048+ bits) for security. Vulnerable to quantum computers running Shor's algorithm. Cannot encrypt data larger than the key.
rsa.realWorld: TLS certificate signatures, PGP/GPG email encryption, code signing, SSH key authentication. Typically used to exchange a symmetric key, not to encrypt data directly.

chachaCompare.howItWorks: ChaCha20 uses a 12-byte (96-bit) nonce; XChaCha20 extends this to 24 bytes (192-bit) by first running HChaCha20 to derive a subkey. The extra nonce space makes random nonce generation safe even at massive scale.
chachaCompare.strengths: XChaCha20's 192-bit nonce makes collision probability negligible — you'd need to encrypt 2^96 messages before a 50% collision chance. ChaCha20's 96-bit nonce requires careful sequential nonce management.
chachaCompare.weaknesses: XChaCha20 adds one HChaCha20 call per message (negligible overhead). ChaCha20 is slightly faster but requires deterministic nonce management to avoid catastrophic nonce reuse.
chachaCompare.realWorld: XChaCha20 is used by libsodium (default AEAD), NaCl, USBVault, and Cloudflare. ChaCha20 is used by WireGuard and TLS 1.3 (where nonce management is built into the protocol).

pbkdf2.howItWorks: Repeatedly applies a pseudorandom function (typically HMAC-SHA256) to the password with a salt. Each iteration feeds the output of the previous one, creating a chain that is intentionally slow to compute.
pbkdf2.strengths: NIST standardized (SP 800-132). Available in WebCrypto and every major platform. Simple, well-analyzed construction. Configurable iteration count for tuning.
pbkdf2.weaknesses: Only CPU-hard (no memory requirement) — vulnerable to GPU and ASIC attacks. An attacker with specialized hardware can test billions of passwords per second despite high iterations.
pbkdf2.realWorld: Wi-Fi WPA2 key derivation, macOS keychain, iOS data protection, .NET Rfc2898DeriveBytes, LastPass (100,100 iterations).

bcrypt.howItWorks: Uses the Blowfish cipher's expensive key setup phase. The cost factor determines work: cost N means 2^N rounds of key expansion. Each round processes 4 KB of data, providing modest memory usage that resists simple parallelization.
bcrypt.strengths: Adaptive — increase the cost factor as hardware gets faster. Built-in salt storage in the hash output. 25+ years of cryptanalysis with no practical attacks found.
bcrypt.weaknesses: Fixed 4 KB memory makes it vulnerable to modern GPU attacks. Limited to 72-byte passwords. The Blowfish base cipher is aging. Not designed for key derivation (outputs a hash, not a key).
bcrypt.realWorld: Ruby on Rails (default), PHP (password_hash), Django, many web frameworks. The standard for password hashing before Argon2.

scrypt.howItWorks: Derives keys using a combination of PBKDF2, Salsa20, and a large memory buffer. The N parameter controls both CPU and memory cost. Processing requires random access to the full memory buffer, making it hard to parallelize.
scrypt.strengths: Memory-hard — attacking with GPUs requires proportional RAM per core, making parallel attacks expensive. Configurable CPU, memory, and parallelism parameters.
scrypt.weaknesses: Memory hardness is not 'time-memory tradeoff resistant' — an attacker can trade memory for extra computation. Parameter tuning is less intuitive than Argon2. No side-channel resistance guarantee.
scrypt.realWorld: Litecoin mining, Tarsnap backup encryption, Ethereum (pre-merge), some cryptocurrency wallets.

argon2i.howItWorks: Fills a large memory array using data-independent access patterns (the access sequence depends only on the parameters, not the password). Multiple passes mix the memory contents. The data-independent pattern prevents side-channel attacks that observe memory access.
argon2i.strengths: Side-channel resistant — an attacker monitoring cache timing or memory access patterns learns nothing about the password. Safe for environments where side-channel attacks are a concern (shared hosting, SGX enclaves).
argon2i.weaknesses: Slightly slower than Argon2d (data-dependent variant) because it cannot optimize memory access patterns. The data-independent access pattern provides weaker time-memory tradeoff resistance.
argon2i.realWorld: Recommended for environments with side-channel concerns. Used in combination as the first pass of Argon2id.

argon2id.howItWorks: Hybrid approach: the first half of the first pass uses Argon2i (data-independent, side-channel resistant), then switches to Argon2d (data-dependent, stronger tradeoff resistance) for remaining passes. Combines the best properties of both.
argon2id.strengths: Winner of the Password Hashing Competition (2015). Resists both GPU/ASIC attacks (memory-hard) and side-channel attacks (Argon2i first pass). Configurable memory, time, and parallelism. USBVault uses: 64 MiB memory, 3 iterations, 4 parallel lanes.
argon2id.weaknesses: Relatively new (2015) compared to bcrypt (1999). Not yet FIPS standardized (under review). Higher memory requirement may be unsuitable for very constrained devices.
argon2id.realWorld: USBVault (default KDF), libsodium (pwhash default), 1Password, Bitwarden, Signal. Recommended by OWASP for new password hashing deployments.

hkdf.howItWorks: Two-phase process: (1) Extract — condenses input key material with a salt using HMAC into a fixed-length pseudorandom key. (2) Expand — derives multiple output keys from the PRK using different 'info' strings for domain separation.
hkdf.strengths: Provably secure under standard assumptions. Domain separation ensures different 'info' strings produce independent keys — a single master key can safely derive keys for encryption, authentication, and backup without cross-contamination.
hkdf.weaknesses: Not a password-based KDF — requires high-entropy input key material. Do not use HKDF directly on passwords (use Argon2id first, then HKDF for subkeys). Output keys are only as strong as the input.
hkdf.realWorld: TLS 1.3 key schedule, Signal Protocol (Double Ratchet), WireGuard, USBVault key hierarchy (derives per-file keys from master encryption key).

help.title: Help & Support
help.ticketSubmittedMessage: We received your request and will respond to {{email}} within 24 hours.
help.subject: Subject
help.gs1Detail1: Navigate to the main Dashboard screen from the sidebar.
help.gs1Detail2: Use the quick action button or the vault table header to start creating a new vault.
help.gs1Detail3: Choose a password with at least 12 characters. The strength meter will guide you.
help.gs1Detail4: Select AES-256-GCM-SIV for standard security or PQC Hybrid for quantum-resistant protection.
help.gs1Detail5: Drag and drop files or use the file picker to add files to your newly created vault.
help.gs2Detail1: Select a vault from the Dashboard vault table to open it.
help.gs2Detail2: Drag files from your computer directly onto the vault view to import them.
help.gs2Detail3: Click the "Add Files" button and browse your device to select files.
help.gs2Detail4: Create folders within your vault to keep files organized and easy to find.
help.gs3Detail1: Open the Settings page and navigate to the Security section.
help.gs3Detail2: Toggle on Face ID, Touch ID, or your platform's biometric option.
help.gs3Detail3: For maximum security, add a FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware key like a YubiKey.
help.gs3Detail4: Lock and unlock your vault to confirm 2FA is working correctly.
help.gs4Detail1: Navigate to the Advanced Security section in Settings.
help.gs4Detail2: Toggle on automatic encrypted backups and choose your preferred frequency.
help.gs4Detail3: Choose Daily, Weekly, or Monthly depending on how often your vault changes.
help.gs4Detail4: Click "Export Encrypted Backup" to download an immediate backup file.
help.gs4Detail5: Keep backup files on a separate drive or cloud storage for disaster recovery.
help.gs5Detail1: Select a vault and navigate to its sharing or collaboration options.
help.gs5Detail2: Type the email address of the person you want to share with.
help.gs5Detail3: Choose between View Only, Edit, or Full Access permissions.
help.gs5Detail4: The collaborator receives an encrypted invitation that only they can accept.
help.gs5Detail5: Revoke or modify access from the vault's sharing panel at any time.
help.subtitle: Find answers, guides, and resources to get the most from USBVault
help.gsDesc1: Step-by-step guide to encrypt and secure your files
help.gsDesc2: Learn how to organize and manage encrypted vaults
help.gsDesc3: Share encrypted files securely with others
help.gsDesc4: Use built-in password manager for secure credentials
help.srDesc2: Detailed explanation of all 10 security layers
help.srDesc3: How to safely backup and store recovery phrase
help.faq1q: How does post-quantum cryptography protect my data?
help.faq1a: Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) uses ML-KEM-1024 (FIPS 203) for hybrid key encapsulation, providing resistance against both classical and quantum computing attacks. Even if quantum computers break RSA and ECC in the future, your data encrypted with PQC remains secure.
help.faq2q: What happens if I lose my recovery phrase?
help.faq2a: Your recovery phrase is the only backup to restore access to your vault. If lost, your vault becomes permanently inaccessible. Always store your recovery phrase in a secure location, separate from your device.
help.faq3q: How is my master password stored?
help.faq3a: Your master password never leaves your device and is never sent to our servers. It's processed through Argon2id KDF and verified using SRP-6a zero-knowledge proof.
help.faq4q: Can I use USBVault on multiple devices?
help.faq4a: Yes, you can install USBVault on multiple devices. Your vault data is synchronized across devices when you use the same credentials.
help.faq5q: What encryption algorithms does USBVault support?
help.faq5a: USBVault uses AES-256-GCM-SIV for file encryption with 64KB streaming chunks. For AEAD, we support both AES-256-GCM-SIV and XChaCha20-Poly1305.
help.faq6a: USBVault has undergone comprehensive third-party security audits. Our implementation follows defense-in-depth principles with 10 layers of security.

onboarding.pqcDescription: Checking your environment for post-quantum cryptography support. USBVault uses ML-KEM-1024 (FIPS 203) hybrid key encapsulation to protect your data against future quantum computing threats.
onboarding.pqcUnavailable: PQC hybrid mode is unavailable on this device. Classical AES-256-GCM-SIV encryption will be used. You can enable PQC on a supported device later.
onboarding.cipherDescription: Select the encryption algorithm for your vault. All options provide military-grade security with authenticated encryption.
onboarding.cipherAesDesc: NIST standard, nonce-misuse resistant AEAD. Recommended for most users.
onboarding.cipherXchachaDesc: Extended nonce AEAD cipher. Preferred for high-throughput streaming.
onboarding.cipherPqcDesc: Post-quantum hybrid combining ML-KEM-1024 with AES-256 for quantum resistance.
onboarding.identityDescription: Your cryptographic identity is used for secure file sharing and message signing. An Ed25519 keypair has been generated.
onboarding.vaultReadyDescription: Your secure vault is configured and ready. Here is a summary of your setup:
onboarding.usbvaultDescription: USBVault is a portable encrypted file vault and password manager with post-quantum cryptography. It secures your files, credentials, and messages with military-grade encryption on any device.
onboarding.usbvaultNotEmail: USBVault is not an email service or email replacement. For private email, we recommend: ProtonMail, Tutanota, or Skiff Mail.
